summary: Gruber follows up on the Dashboard-vs.-Konfabulator controversy, clarifying that his criticism targeted the bystanders crying foul rather than Konfabulator's creators personally. He systematically dismantles arguments that Apple 'ripped off' Konfabulator by tracing Dashboard's lineage through desk accessories and HyperCard, arguing that none of the constituent ideas — scriptable runtimes, single-purpose applets, skinnable UIs — were novel to Konfabulator. He acknowledges Brent Simmons's concern that Apple bundling similar functionality discourages third-party developers, but counters that Apple buys technology and implementations, not ideas. He concludes that Dashboard's real significance is making the Mac a platform for client-side web applications, built on open standards anyone already knows.
key_points: Dashboard's lineage traces directly through desk accessories (scope) and HyperCard (ease of creation), both predating Konfabulator by years or decades.
Konfabulator's widget aesthetics derive from the broader skinning/theming movement pioneered by Winamp and others, not from any Konfabulator innovation.
Apple routinely acquires technology it considers superior (Final Cut, SoundJam/iTunes, etc.) but has no obligation to pay for ideas alone.
Apple's adoption of 'widgets' over 'gadgets' was unnecessarily antagonistic and contributed to the perception of copying.
Dashboard's use of web standards (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) makes it fundamentally more inclusive than Konfabulator's proprietary platform, which is the real story being missed.
money_quotes: Making it easier to write client-side software using scripting languages is clearly something that makes Mac OS X a better platform. That Konfabulator offered something similar first doesn't entitle them to anything.
I see Konfabulator as a well done implementation of existing ideas. If you see it as something deeply innovative, then we've reached a point where we'll have to respectfully disagree.
What Apple doesn't pay for are ideas. I suppose Simmons's argument is that it might be a good idea if they did — but I'm unaware of any company that does.
If Apple saw Konfabulator as a good idea but not a good implementation — what would they have been paying for? Good will? Karma?
Where does the line get drawn? Should Apple have bought the rights to iCab before shipping Safari?

Whatever the reason for Apple's apparent switch to and appropriation of 'widget', it's unfortunate. To the casual observer, it certainly contributes to the perception that Dashboard is Konfabulator knock-off, and, worse, it's unnecessarily antagonistic.
Arlo Rose is a hyper-talented visual designer; his widgets possess a certain je ne sais quoi. But they're still just skins, albeit extraordinarily well-done ones.
key_insight: Konfabulator assembled existing ideas well but invented nothing new, and Apple's real contribution with Dashboard is opening client-side app development to anyone who knows web standards.
